questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with fatigue and jaundice for 2 weeks. She has no other symptoms and no risk factors for viral hepatitis. Examination reveals icteric sclera and mild hepatomegaly. Investigations: bilirubin 85 µmol/L, ALT 450 U/L, ALP 150 U/L, IgG 22 g/L, ANA positive 1:640, anti-smooth muscle antibody positive. Serology for hepatitis A, B, C, and E negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Positive ANA and anti-smooth muscle antibodies, elevated IgG, and transaminase-predominant liver injury in a young woman are classic for autoimmune hepatitis. Primary biliary cholangitis typically features anti-mitochondrial antibodies and cholestatic LFTs. Drug-induced liver injury requires a culprit drug history.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with intermittent jaundice and pruritus over 3 months. LFTs show fluctuating transaminases (ALT 200-400 U/L) and mildly elevated ALP. Liver biopsy shows interface hepatitis with plasma cells. Viral serology negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Interface hepatitis with plasma cells is characteristic of autoimmune hepatitis. Primary biliary cholangitis would show florid bile duct lesions and granulomas. Overlap syndromes are less common. Chronic hepatitis B would have positive HBsAg.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to A&E with acute confusion and jaundice. She has no history of liver disease but took paracetamol for a headache 48 hours ago (8 g in 24 hours). She is encephalopathic with asterixis. INR 3.2, ALT 3500 U/L, bilirubin 200 µmol/L. Paracetamol level is subtherapeutic. ANA and anti-smooth muscle antibodies are positive. Anti-HAV IgM negative, anti-HBc IgM negative, anti-HEV IgM negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Wilson disease with fulminant liver failure
Autoimmune hepatitis presenting as acute liver failure
questions.explanation: Despite paracetamol use, the subtherapeutic level and positive autoantibodies suggest autoimmune hepatitis as the cause of severe acute liver failure. Paracetamol toxicity typically has a higher serum level. Wilson disease is less common and would have low ceruloplasmin; Budd-Chiari presents with painful hepatomegaly and imaging findings.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with abdominal distension and lower limb swelling over 2 weeks. She has no known liver disease but reports fatigue for months. Examination reveals ascites and spider naevi. LFTs: ALT 80 U/L, ALP 120 U/L, bilirubin 30 µmol/L, albumin 28 g/L, INR 1.5. IgG 20 g/L, ANA positive, anti-smooth muscle positive. Ultrasound shows a shrunken liver with nodular surface and ascites. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Decompensated cirrhosis due to autoimmune hepatitis
questions.explanation: Positive autoantibodies and elevated IgG in a patient with decompensated cirrhosis, without other risk factors, strongly suggest autoimmune hepatitis as the underlying aetiology. PBC would typically have anti-mitochondrial antibodies; alcoholic and NASH lack serological markers.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks gestation presents with jaundice, dark urine, and pruritus. She has no hypertension or proteinuria. LFTs: ALT 350 U/L, ALP 200 U/L, bilirubin 80 µmol/L, bile acids 50 µmol/L (mildly elevated). ANA positive 1:160, anti-smooth muscle positive. Serum IgG 18 g/L. Viral hepatitis serology negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Positive ANA and anti-smooth muscle antibodies with elevated IgG indicate autoimmune hepatitis, which can present during pregnancy. Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy typically has higher bile acids and normal IgG; HELLP includes hypertension and thrombocytopenia; acute fatty liver often has hypoglycaemia and coagulopathy.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with jaundice, fatigue, and raised transaminases (ALT 400 IU/L). Viral hepatitis serology is negative. She drinks no alcohol. Which single serological marker is most specific for confirming autoimmune hepatitis?
questions.explanation: Anti-smooth muscle antibody is the most specific serological marker for autoimmune hepatitis type 1. AMA is specific for primary biliary cholangitis, anti-LKM1 for type 2 AIH (more common in children), ANA is non-specific, and IgG is elevated but not diagnostic alone.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is found to have mild transaminitis (ALT 65 IU/L) on routine blood tests. He is asymptomatic. Autoantibody screen (ANA, SMA, AMA) is negative. IgG is within normal range. Viral serology is negative. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis of autoimmune hepatitis?
questions.explanation: In seronegative or atypical presentations of autoimmune hepatitis, liver biopsy is essential to demonstrate interface hepatitis and exclude other causes. Anti-LKM1 may be positive in type 2 AIH but is less likely in this age group. Other options are for alternative diagnoses.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with known autoimmune hepatitis on azathioprine develops pruritus and an elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP 350 IU/L). Her bilirubin and transaminases are near normal. Which autoantibody should be checked to investigate for an overlap syndrome?
questions.explanation: Pruritus and raised ALP in a patient with AIH suggests overlap with primary biliary cholangitis (PBC). Anti-mitochondrial antibody is highly specific for PBC. SMA suggests active AIH, and anti-LKM1 for type 2 AIH. ANCA is associated with primary sclerosing cholangitis.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman presents at 20 weeks of pregnancy with jaundice and raised transaminases (ALT 350 IU/L). She has no prior liver disease. Which investigation is safest and most likely to confirm autoimmune hepatitis in this scenario?
questions.explanation: Pregnancy is a relative contraindication to liver biopsy due to risk of bleeding and fetal harm. Anti-smooth muscle antibody is a specific serological test for AIH and can be performed safely. Ultrasound and MRCP/ERCP are not diagnostic for AIH and carry risks (radiation/contrast).

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman is investigated for abnormal LFTs. ANA is positive at 1:80 titre. IgG is 19 g/L (normal <16). Liver biopsy shows interface hepatitis with plasma cell infiltration. Viral serology is negative. Using the simplified autoimmune hepatitis scoring system, what is her total score?
questions.explanation: Simplified AIH score: ANA ≥1:80 = 2 points; IgG >1.1 x ULN (19/16 = 1.1875) = 2 points; histology with interface hepatitis and plasma cells is 'typical' = 2 points; no viral hepatitis = 1 point. Total = 2+2+2+1 = 7, indicating definite AIH.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with fatigue and jaundice. LFTs: ALT 450 U/L, AST 380 U/L, ALP 120 U/L, bilirubin 60 μmol/L. ANA positive, anti-smooth muscle antibody positive. Liver biopsy shows interface hepatitis. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Prednisolone 30 mg daily plus azathioprine 1 mg/kg/day
questions.explanation: First-line therapy for autoimmune hepatitis is dual therapy with prednisolone and azathioprine (or mycophenolate if azathioprine contraindicated). Budesonide is an alternative only in non-cirrhotic mild disease, but first-line remains prednisolone plus azathioprine.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man presents with acute onset jaundice and malaise. LFTs: ALT 1200 U/L, AST 1100 U/L, ALP 80 U/L, bilirubin 250 μmol/L, INR 1.8. IgG elevated, anti-LKM1 negative, ANA positive. No encephalopathy. What is the most appropriate initial medical therapy?
options: Prednisolone 40 mg daily plus azathioprine 1 mg/kg/day
questions.explanation: In acute severe autoimmune hepatitis without encephalopathy, high-dose prednisolone (40-60 mg daily) is first-line. Adding azathioprine is avoided initially due to risk of severe hepatotoxicity. IV methylprednisolone is reserved for cases with fulminant features, and transplantation is only if no response to medical therapy.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with autoimmune hepatitis is found to have leucopenia (WBC 2.8 × 10^9/L) and thrombocytopenia (platelets 90 × 10^9/L) before starting treatment. Which drug should be avoided as part of first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: Azathioprine can cause myelosuppression, which is particularly risky in patients with pre-existing cytopenia. Prednisolone is safe, and mycophenolate or tacrolimus can be considered as alternatives if azathioprine is contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with autoimmune hepatitis has had incomplete biochemical response (ALT persistently 200 U/L) after 6 months of prednisolone and azathioprine. She is unable to tolerate higher doses of prednisolone. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: In patients with inadequate response to first-line therapy (prednisolone + azathioprine), NICE recommends adding mycophenolate mofetil as second-line. Tacrolimus and cyclosporine are third-line options. Budesonide is not effective in this setting.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with autoimmune hepatitis is on maintenance azathioprine 100 mg daily and prednisolone 5 mg daily. She is planning a pregnancy. Which drug is absolutely contraindicated due to teratogenicity?
questions.explanation: Mycophenolate mofetil is teratogenic and absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. Azathioprine is relatively safe and can be continued at low doses. Prednisolone, budesonide, and cyclosporine are not teratogenic and can be used if needed.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with fatigue, jaundice, and abdominal discomfort. LFTs: ALT 450 U/L, ALP 120 U/L, bilirubin 85 μmol/L. IgG 22 g/L, ANA 1:320, anti-smooth muscle antibody 1:160. Viral serology negative. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Liver biopsy is recommended to confirm the diagnosis and assess fibrosis before initiating immunosuppression. Starting prednisolone empirically is not first-line without biopsy unless contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with autoimmune hepatitis on prednisolone 15 mg and azathioprine 100 mg daily presents with insomnia, tremor, and weight gain. LFTs are normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer for urgent liver transplant assessment
questions.explanation: The symptoms are classic of steroid toxicity; reducing the prednisolone dose is the first step while monitoring disease activity. Propranolol is symptomatic but does not address the cause.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with no known liver disease presents with acute jaundice, confusion, and bruising. LFTs: ALT 1500 U/L, bilirubin 400 μmol/L, INR 2.2. IgG 25 g/L, ANA positive. She is drowsy but rousable. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: The patient has acute severe autoimmune hepatitis with hepatic encephalopathy and coagulopathy, meeting King's College criteria for urgent transplantation. Immediate referral to a transplant centre is the priority.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known autoimmune hepatitis cirrhosis, compensated, on azathioprine. Routine surveillance ultrasound shows a 3 cm liver lesion. Serum AFP is 200 ng/mL. He is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform liver biopsy of the lesion
questions.explanation: A 3 cm lesion with AFP >200 in a cirrhotic liver is highly suspicious for hepatocellular carcinoma. Triple-phase CT is the next step for diagnosis and staging per NICE guidelines. Liver biopsy is reserved for uncertain cases.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 16 weeks gestation with known autoimmune hepatitis well-controlled on prednisolone 10 mg and azathioprine 50 mg daily. She presents with rising transaminases (ALT 200 U/L, AST 180 U/L) and pruritus. Bilirubin normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, autoimmune hepatitis flares are treated with increased prednisolone as first line. Mycophenolate is teratogenic and contraindicated. Although pruritus raises concern for intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy, the raised transaminases suggest an AIH flare is more likely, and steroid adjustment is the immediate priority.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with jaundice, fatigue, and arthralgia. LFTs show ALT 800 U/L, bilirubin 180 µmol/L, ALP normal. ANA and anti-smooth muscle antibodies positive. Which single finding most urgently indicates that she requires hospital admission?
questions.explanation: An INR of 1.6 indicates significant synthetic dysfunction in the context of acute hepatitis, raising concern for acute liver failure. While ALT>1000 and bilirubin>200 are markers of severity, an elevated INR is a more direct indicator of hepatic decompensation and need for admission.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with autoimmune hepatitis maintained on prednisolone 5 mg and azathioprine 50 mg is seen in clinic. He feels well but ALT is 120 U/L (ULN 40) and bilirubin 25 µmol/L. Which single finding in history or examination would be a red flag for decompensation?
questions.explanation: New ascites is a sign of portal hypertension and decompensation in cirrhosis, requiring urgent assessment and admission. Spider naevi and palmar erythema are stigmata of chronic liver disease but do not indicate acute decompensation.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with known autoimmune hepatitis (non-adherent to treatment) presents with confusion, asterixis, and jaundice. LFTs: ALT 2000 U/L, bilirubin 400 µmol/L, INR 2.5. Which additional finding would most strongly indicate the need for liver transplant assessment?
questions.explanation: Acute liver failure with hepatorenal syndrome (creatinine >200) is a poor prognostic sign and indication for transplant assessment. Although lactate and ammonia may be elevated, renal impairment in this context signals multiorgan failure and urgency.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with autoimmune hepatitis and cirrhosis presents after a single episode of haematemesis (small volume, now stable). OGD shows oesophageal varices (grade 2) with red wale marks. Which single admission finding is most concerning?
options: Postural hypotension (drop >20 mmHg systolic on standing)
questions.explanation: Postural hypotension indicates significant intravascular volume depletion from variceal bleeding, necessitating urgent resuscitation. Red wale marks predict high rebleeding risk, and postural hypotension is a physiological warning of decompensation.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with autoimmune hepatitis (diagnosed 5 years ago, in remission on azathioprine) is 30 weeks pregnant. She presents with nausea, right upper quadrant pain, and mild jaundice. LFTs: ALT 300 U/L, bilirubin 80 µmol/L, ALP 200 U/L (pregnancy-adjusted normal). Which finding most strongly suggests an AIH flare rather than a pregnancy-specific liver disorder?
questions.explanation: Elevated serum IgG is characteristic of autoimmune hepatitis and, in the context of high transaminases, suggests a flare. Elevated bile acids indicate intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy; anti-mitochondrial antibody suggests PBC; thrombocytopenia and hypertension point toward pre-eclampsia or HELLP.
